questions.question_text: Under an exclusive right-to-sell listing, the broker earns a commission if:
questions.correct_answer: The property sells, no matter who finds the buyer
distractors: Only if the broker personally finds the buyer
Only if the property sold through the MLS
Only if the seller accepts the full price
questions.explanation: An exclusive right-to-sell listing guarantees the broker's commission regardless of who procures the buyer — even if the seller finds the buyer independently.

questions.question_text: A Colorado listing agreement must include a definite:
questions.explanation: Listing agreements must include a definite expiration date. Open-ended listings that continue indefinitely are not permitted.

questions.question_text: A listing contract includes a holdover clause. This clause must be:
questions.correct_answer: Limited to prospects the broker negotiated with and named in writing to the seller
distractors: Approved by the Real Estate Commission
questions.explanation: Under Commission Rule 6.14.E, a holdover provision may entitle the firm to a commission after expiration only for persons or properties the broker negotiated with or on during the listing term AND whose names or addresses were submitted in writing to the seller during the term (including extensions).

questions.question_text: Under an exclusive AGENCY listing, a seller who finds the buyer entirely on their own owes:
questions.explanation: Under an exclusive agency listing, the broker is the seller's only broker but the seller reserves the right to sell without owing a commission. This contrasts with the exclusive right-to-sell, where the broker earns the commission no matter who procures the buyer.

questions.question_text: As of January 1, 2026, listing contracts no longer include paragraph 5.3. This change affects:
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5.3 was removed from listing contracts effective January 1, 2026. Brokers must now obtain clear written authorization before sharing confidential information with third parties.

questions.question_text: Under MLS rules adopted after the NAR settlement (August 17, 2024), MLS participants working with a buyer must have, before touring a home:
distractors: A pre-qualification letter from a lender
questions.explanation: The requirement comes from national MLS policy adopted under the NAR settlement (effective August 17, 2024), not from Colorado license law: MLS participants must enter a written buyer agreement before touring a home with a buyer, including live virtual tours.

questions.question_text: A buyer representation agreement must specify broker compensation that is:
distractors: At least 3% of the purchase price
Set by the local REALTOR board rate
Determined after the closing is complete
questions.explanation: The agreement must specify compensation that is objectively ascertainable and not open-ended. It must also state that commissions are negotiable and not set by law.

questions.question_text: A buyer representative agreement must conspicuously disclose that:
distractors: The buyer will pay all closing costs
The MLS sets minimum commission rates
The broker guarantees finding a home
questions.explanation: The agreement must include a conspicuous statement that broker commissions are not set by law and are fully negotiable.

questions.question_text: Under a buyer rep agreement, the broker may not receive compensation from any source that exceeds:
questions.correct_answer: The amount agreed to with the buyer
questions.explanation: The buyer agreement must state that the broker may not receive compensation exceeding the agreed amount, regardless of what other sources may offer.

questions.question_text: A broker wants to show a buyer a home during a virtual tour. Under current rules, the broker needs:
questions.correct_answer: A written buyer agreement before the tour
distractors: Only a verbal agreement for virtual tours
No agreement until an in-person visit
A written agreement only after an offer
questions.explanation: The written buyer agreement requirement applies to both in-person and live virtual tours. A virtual tour is treated the same as an in-person showing.

questions.question_text: Which element is required in every Colorado listing contract?
questions.explanation: Listing contracts must include the amount or method of calculating compensation, a definite expiration date, and property identification.

questions.question_text: Before performing any brokerage services for a seller, a Colorado listing contract must be:
questions.explanation: Commission Rule 6.14 requires listing contracts to be in writing before the broker performs any services requiring a license, and to include a definite termination date. No notarization, filing, or recording is required.

questions.question_text: A listing contract must identify the property. This typically includes:
distractors: Only the subdivision or plat name
questions.explanation: Proper property identification typically includes the address and may include the legal description. Using only a partial identifier may not adequately identify the property.

distractors: Forms from the local REALTOR board only
questions.explanation: Colorado requires the use of Commission-approved forms for listing contracts. Custom or third-party forms are not permitted for standard transactions.

questions.question_text: A seller asks the broker to create a custom listing contract with unique terms. The broker should:
questions.correct_answer: Use the approved form and add provisions
distractors: Draft a custom contract from scratch
Have the seller draft the contract
questions.explanation: The broker should use the Commission-approved listing form and add unique terms in the additional provisions section. Drafting a custom contract from scratch would be unauthorized practice of law.
